But we cannot use grep in >. Since we can only use run_cmd one command at a time. Thus we must not run sysctl in the fix script. We need to apply runtime change via systemctl reload? On RHEL you can run sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-ipv6-forwarding.conf after writing. But earlier attempt used echo > which probably didn't apply due to file missing? Also maybe the verification expects that file must be named 10-something to be applied after earlier files. Let's produce a file and have it applied by sysctl. Let's choose path: /etc/sysctl.d/10-disable-ipv6-forwarding.conf to override earlier? It might still apply. But maybe earlier ones override? Actually sysctl loads in numeric order; 10 comes first. If earlier file also sets forwarding=1 then 10 sets to 0 might not override. We want to ensure this setting overrides earlier. So we should use a high number like 99. But earlier attempt used 99 file but verification failed. Why? Possibly because the file didn't have correct syntax? They included newline with comment block lines... It had lines with '#' comment. That should be okay. Why did verification fail? Might be because system expects not to use spaces around '='? But they used spaces. sysctl syntax: key value separated by space or "="? Usually `net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding = 0` is fine. Maybe the comment lines H? It's fine. But why didn't scan pass? Maybe because it didn't run sysctl -p? The runtime didn't change. The runtime will be applied only if sysctl uses sysctl -p or systemd's sysctl service. But they didn't apply runtime. So scanning didn't see change. So we must apply config runtime after writing. But we can't run sysctl -w but we can run sysctl -p to load the file. That will apply runtime. So plan: create file if not exist or modify existing file to set. Then run sysctl -p to apply. Also we need to comment out any other occurrences. So improved plan: use run_cmd 'grep -rl net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf' to find files containing the key. Then comment out lines in each using sed -i 's/^\s*net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding\b/#&/' file. But we can only run one command at a time. So we might need to run separate commands. But we can run grep to get list, then read_file each file to see if we need to modify. Simpler: just create a new file 99-disable-ipv6-forwarding.conf that sets the parameter, regardless of existing entries. Systemd sysctl will load all files; any duplicate keys will override earlier ones in order. So the last one with higher number wins. So using 99 ensures overriding earlier. So we need not comment out existing. So just create file 99-disable-ipv6-forwarding.conf with content. Then run sysctl -p to apply at runtime. That is different from previous attempt using > because earlier attempt used > and didn't apply runtime. But they used echo -e > earlier. That didn't apply runtime.
